title: 2026/05/11 [Part 3]
committee: Kay Jones Alexander Kerr New Zealand Council for Civil Liberties Thomas Beagle, Chair New Zealand Law Society Te Kahui Ture o Aotearoa Nick Chisnall, Criminal Law Committee
description: Monday, 11 May 2026 | Justice Subcommittee B Policing Amendment Bill Hearing of Evidence Submitters: Kay Jones Alexander Kerr New Zealand Council for Civil Liberties Thomas Beagle, Chair New Zealand Law Society Te Kahui Ture o Aotearoa Nick Chisnall, Criminal Law Committee convenor Juliet Philpott, Human Rights and Privacy Committee member Community Law Centres Aotearoa Karen Hodgson, Law Reform Coordinator Privacy Foundation New Zealand Incorporated Katrine Evans, Chair, Privacy Foundation NZ Police Association Steve Watt, President Wellington Community Justice Project - Howard League Submissions Team Cordelia Repail (student volunteer) Maivish Khalid (student volunteer) Jane Hong (student volunteer) Justice For All Inc Lois McGirr, Secretary Janice Brewer, Treasurer Shane Herdman, Member People Against Prisons Aotearoa Tom Pearce, Parliamentary Advocacy Coordinator Miro Thomas Ireson Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Psychiatrists Dr John Gregson PILLAR NZ Arian Tashakkori, Head of Operations Nathan Seiuli, Executive Director BIPOC Legal Collective Aotearoa Shaymaa Arif, Founder Mana Mokopuna - Children's Commissioner Dr Claire Achmad

chunks.text: Belongings knows how important it is to actually to know where everything is. Because do you either set a torch to the whole thing, or do you find the treasure? Without that system, you can't manage the information well. Now, we don't want surveillance, but we also don't want information that's collected to not be able to be managed.

Now, jumping briefly to what's happening in other parts of the world. In the US, there has been overreach of taking data on lots of different systems and feeding them into different unrelated systems so that ICE, the immigration homeland security group, have been arresting people on the basis of they've entered onto a list. Currently, there's a New Zealand-born woman who has a green card in the US, Evalee Wehiongi, who has been pinged partly because, there was a record of her having potentially had a

minor criminal offense that was wiped some years ago. With the majority of data servers being owned by or run by American companies, the more New Zealand data that is put into systems that are not under our control, the more the risk is that that information will be taken elsewhere.

I don't think that we can risk having vast additional amounts of visual material that could be used as AI training systems going into other people, and that includes American companies' hands. So that's a whole bundle of different things, and I'm happy to talk about anything that's in my submissions, if there's any questions.

Yeah. We've just got about a minute there, so we'll just open it up. If Carl or Dan, have you got any questions of Kaye? I suppose as an ordinary person reading this bill, it's not entirely obvious how these powers would work day to day. And my question is, what are the most important details do you think are missing that Parliament needs before passing this law?

Well, for any type of data collection, you need to have storage and retention and deletion policies. You also need to have ability to know how to access it. So for example, if at some stage there was web cameras on police, can people access that as well? Can people get photos that have been taken of themselves?

How easy would that be to be? How can we be sure also that there is training in not being preemptively... We want intelligence, not surveillance. We don't want a police constable on the court saying, "Oh, I took those photos, Your Honor, because they looked suspicious." Talking about a group of young Maori and, the whole appearance of racism, even if that is not the motivation, is something that in some communities lessens the ability of communities to trust and cooperate with the police. And what we want as an outcome is

a safer Aotearoa with communities actually sort of saying, "It's that guy." Like in my community group, we've got, currently there's somebody who takes their dog for walks, doesn't have dog bags, and there's people are tagging them saying, "This is a problem." And people are saying, "Does anyone have webcams that might capture this so we might be able to track down and let the council deal with it?" But at the same time, it's like not trying to be evil or beat up the guy.

It's trying to find a safe way of dealing with a public nuisance. And the police should be dealing with more things than just public nuisance. Not who's tagging that wall at the local park, but actually who's breaking into cars, who's sort of attacking the local dairy or whatever.

We want it proportioned, and we want cooperation, and this does not have enough safeguards for people to feel that they will be trusting the police. Okay, Kaye. Thank you. I think Carl possibly had a question. But has that answered that for you, Carl? Yeah, I think I know you've got to move on chair, so that's okay. Okay. Thank you very much. Thank you very much for your submission today- Thank you... Kaye. Thank you. Our next submitter is by Zoom, Alexandra Kerr.

So we'll just wait for Alexandra. Alexandra, are you there? We're looking at a keyboard at the moment. Maybe if you could turn the camera around or if you could speak. That's better. Just up a little bit. Perfect. Alexandra Kerr, welcome. You've got five minutes with us today.

We'll let you go straight into your submission. Thank you. Can you hear me? Yes, we can. Yes. Okay. Because I can't actually see you. For some reason, it's all disappeared. I can definitely see you. We can hear you. So, as long as you're confident there, you're- I can make my submission. Yep. All right.

Okay, thank you very much. Obviously, this is about the expansion of police powers. I know that the last speaker talked a lot about data collection, but I think this is about a general increase in police powers. And my big concern is that, according to the Policing Act 2008, police are essentially empowered to report a breach of the peace.

In other words, they are peacekeepers. And a lot of what I see today is going beyond that without any oversight. And so the expansion of these powers without fur- withoutEqual oversight to me seems like the beginning of a police state. And I don't want to make remarks that sound all dramatic without providing some sort of information, but I was looking at a video the other day of three police officers that stopped a lady, she was maybe 20. Nice looking thing. And then

a third car pulls over, a police officer walks over to her, he says something to her, she obviously mouths off to him, and he smashes her in the face and drops her. And this video is available. I can submit it. The sergeant on the scene, he was identified as the sergeant, walks over.

As far as I know, no, nothing has happened of this. It's just all hushed up. People are treated this way. It's coercive. I'm going to move on to another example. I know you'd like to address that. Another example is the roadside testing at the moment. I've always been big on bodily autonomy. The Bill of Rights protects that right of mine.

However, if a policeman offers to put a reagent inside your mouth, right, and you say, "Look, I don't want that inside my mouth. Give me the material data safety sheets to show that this is actually going to be okay. Show me the health and safety that's gone into it.

And also, could you please sign this public questionnaire saying that you'll take personal liability should something happen to me as a result, like an allergic response or something like that?" And what happens in this situation is if you refuse this test, automatically you're fined $400, you're parked up for 12 hours, you are guilty, and all you've done is try to exercise your basic rights. I am not a drug taker, but I do not intend to have anybody stick anything in my mouth.

So, that is coercive policing. I wouldn't want to see an expansion of powers like that, because it's just over the top without any oversight. You turn around, you ask for their badge number, and all of a sudden you're seen as some kind of, what, sovereign citizen or agitator or something, and they may, in their discretion, treat you even worse.

You may end up at the police station or something, all for the sake of coercive police policy in the first instance. Now, all I would like to point out from this point on, those two examples, I can elaborate on them at any time, is that we need full, accurate, and verifiable records for all actions. We need oversight.

I've written a couple of things here. I mean, the police- Alexandro, what we might do, we just might see if there's any questions. And if not, I'll give you 30 seconds to sum up. But is Carl or Dan, any questions? So we've got no questions. So if you, we've got 30, if you want to sum up the submission just before you leave, please.

It's really bad that Carl has no questions. It means my submission will be dismissed possibly. Look, I just want to sum up by saying that I respect the police, I respect that there is a need for the police, and I have friends in the police force, and I have clients in the police force, and I certainly wouldn't disrespect them by trying to tarnish the whole thing. However, some of these policies are already out of whack with oversight.

In relation to oversight, you don't want to win this stuff in a court of law. What happens at the roadside, what happens at wherever it is, the officers going in there need to know that they have judiciary responsibility. Look, I- We'll cut you there, Alexandro. Thank you very much for your submission today, and we've got your submission also in front of us.

So thank you. Our next submitter is also by Zoom, is the New Zealand Council for Civil Liberties. We'll just see if we can get them online. They're online. Thomas Spiegel, I believe, chair. Have you got Thomas? Welcome, Thomas. You've got 10 minutes with us today. We're in your hands.

Good afternoon. My name's Thomas Spiegel, as you said, I'm the chair of the New Zealand Council for Civil Liberties. Policing Amendment is such a bland name for a bill, but it's one of the biggest grabs of police power in the nation's history. I believe it's a radical bill, and it's a dangerous bill. I'm not going to go through our entire submission, but I do want to talk about the two parts of the bill and what they have in common.

Now, you may be surprised to hear that I'm going to start with police body cameras, because I don't even know. Did you know that you're considering a bill that provides the legal basis for the police to start using body cameras? Because in the words of the bill, "A police employee may record, by any means, visual images of any person or thing that is in or that can be observed."

Okay. What was the allowable purposes this can be done for including safety, integrity, intelligence gathering? It's very apparent that this bill has been designed to provide the legal backing for the use of body camerasNow, at the NZCCL, we're aware that the Police Commissioner has stated he wants to implement body cameras for police.

So we've been doing some research to work out how we can do it safely. We're looking at different schemes in other countries, various requirements about when body cameras can be worn, when they should be turned on, when they can be turned off, who gets access to footage, et cetera. The issues are surprisingly tricky, but I believe not insurmountable.

We also believe that the people of New Zealand, through both consultation and the deliberations of the elected parliament, should have some say in how police select implement body cameras. This bill just skips past all that and says the police can record whatever they like. Pass this law and neither the people nor you MPs will get to say anything about body cameras at all.

Furthermore, the bill enables police or police employees in any public place to use any tool they like in order to record audio or video. This includes such things as high-power zoom lenses, parabolic long-range microphones, and even more advanced technology such as thermal imaging. These tools allow the invasion of people's private places from public places in a way that is incompatible with privacy, and I believe the Search and Surveillance Act.

And it allows these tools to be left unattended and running for a long time. The bill is a green flag to use number plate recognition, facial recognition, live surveillance, drones, gait analysis, cameras and microphones everywhere. Everything's allowed by this bill. Now I want to switch to part two, the road and any other area closure part.

This has expanded a part of the law that police can use to close roads. It allows them to close any accessible area, which pretty well covers most of the country. It also expands the reasons the areas can be closed. While the puffery at the start of the bill talks about antisocial road use, our reading is the text that one or more of the following apply means it applies in all of the other circumstances as well.

It seems the bill will allow police to shut down any area, remove the people for a number of reasons, including they believe that public disorder is imminent, or that a group of people may be expected to cause excessive noise from a vehicle. Now, these aren't the totality of the reasons listed, but I've picked out these because firstly, they deal with things that haven't actually happened at the time the order was made.

As someone who believes that people should be allowed to go about their lives freely as much as possible, this seems very wrong to me. Now we not only have to avoid breaking the law, we also have to make sure we don't give the impression that we might break the law. The second reason I've picked them out is that those descriptions also apply to political protest.

Normal political protest in a democracy is often loud, at least somewhat disorderly, and sometimes involves people using loudspeakers or music systems mounted on vehicles. It seems to me the powers in this bill will be able to use to shut down political demonstrations, order people to disperse, and arrest them if they refuse or try to return. And this is a big problem for a rights-respecting democracy.

Now, to finish off, I want to talk about what these two parts have in common, and what it is is that there are no meaningful restrictions on the use of these powers. There's no exceptions. There's no oversight. There's no approval process or requirement for a senior officer to be involved. There's no appeal. There are really no limits.

The bill embodies a view that the police will never misuse or abuse their powers, that every police officer and employee, because it covers employees as well, will always do the right thing, that we don't need to limit how police powers are used. I believe this is a very dangerous view. Police are human. They make mistakes. They exhibit bias.

Some of them are unfortunately corrupt. And for one obvious and exhaustively documented example, police statistics and reports clearly show they're biased against Māori. A society that enacts laws that the police are always right is going down a very dangerous path towards authoritarianism, and that's why I say it's a radical bill. It's a radical extension of police powers, and it's radical in the way that it assumes that police can do no wrong.

I believe that we should not pass it in its current form. And that's not to say the purpose of the bill is inherently wrong. I believe there is room to better codify how, when, where, and why police can record audio and video. I believe there may be room to give police the ability to close areas where large-scale lawlessness is happening. But these purposes have to be taken seriously in the context of a rights-respecting democratic society that values freedom, which is a version of Aotearoa that I'd like to live in.

They need proper consideration of how we can let police do their job while still protecting our rights, not this carte blanche do what you want license. I recommend you send this bill back for a substantial rewrite. If you don't, please consider some of the suggestions we made in our written submission. Thank you for your time. I'm happy to answer any questions.

Thank you, Thomas. You have got still some time, and there is a question from Dan Rosewarne. So if you- Yeah. Thank you. Thank you, Thomas. Just a question around recommendation two of your submission, where you suggest rewording the new Section 45A, and it's got, "Police constables may make recordings in public without warrant only when there is a reason to believe that there'll be use of force or when violent or aggressive behavior is anticipated or displayed." My question is, isn't that still quite broad? Because the reason I ask is the way I read it, it would

still allow everyday interactions to be recorded just in case something becomes useful later, which is one of your concerns, and I'm just wondering if you had any further thoughts on that at all. I think that was partly inspired by some of the work we're reading about body cameras, and I think it's actually meant to be limiting it to that. That may be slightly overly restrictive, to be honest.

I think it might be more than we'd say we'd want to go. But it's coming from that view of that-We do want to be able to set limits and reasons and requirements for when these things are being recorded, when recording is happening. And I think that's really the most important point to take out of that. We also have another question from Carl Bates.

Thanks very much, Chair. Accepting, Thomas, that part of what you're referring to about being able to have surveillance into closed spaces, parking that for a moment, in relation to your concerns around surveillance in open spaces, particularly around digital videoing and the like, where we live in a world where someone could be standing there and videoing the whole thing anyway- Mm-hmm... why is it such a concern that the police may, under this act, have the ability to also video that in a scenario where that could then be used later as evidence?

Mm-hmm. Yep. I think the thing is that there is a power imbalance there. If a normal person is videoing me doing something, it's an equal power balance. When the police do things, they are acting with the power of the state, and it's intimidating. One of the examples I give is that talking to people who are at protests, this is actually a few years ago now, and they were saying that the police at these political protests standing back using long-lens cameras to zoom in and take photos of everyone there. And they found that intimidating,

and it actually made them feel that they didn't want to turn up at future political protests. So it was discouraging their political participation. Now you can say, "Okay, but journalists might have been doing that too." And the answer is that the people would not have been nervous about journalists doing it. It was the fact that the state was doing it that caused the issue.

And that applies across all these things. The actual rules we come up with, I've got opinions on what some of those should be and so on, but we do have to consider there's power imbalance, and that's why we need separate rules for police. Thank you, Thomas, very much for your time today and your submission. So thank you. We now need to head on to our next submitter- Thank you... also by Zoom. Thank you, Thomas.

The New Zealand Law Society, and I think we may have Juliet Philpott and Nick Chisnell. Welcome. You have 10 minutes with us today. Thank you. Yes. Tena koutou katoa. I'm Nick Chisnell KC. I'm the convener of the Law Society's Criminal Law Committee, and I'm joined by Juliet Philpott, who's a member of the Human Rights and Privacy Committee.

I intend to make some preliminary comments before asking Juliet to address you on Part 1 of the bill. I'll then briefly address Part 2 before we answer any questions that the committee may have for us. Thank you. My starting point is that the Law Society joins the Privacy Commissioner and others who have expressed deep concerns about the significant proposed expansion to police intelligence gathering powers in Part 1 of the bill. For Part 2, we query the justification for and workability of the proposed new powers to enforce the temporary closure of publicly accessible areas.

If the bill was to proceed, we'd invite greater safeguards be put in place. So simply put, the Law Society does not consider that the bill should pass in its current form. The policy justifications for the extensions that the bill proposes to law enforcement powers are questionable and have not been sufficiently articulated.

The lack of public consultation has produced a set of proposals that risk overreach and which are incompatible with settled privacy principles and freedom of expression. In our submission, if enacted, the bill will create real difficulties in application for police officers tasked with using its powers.

Inevitably, litigation will ensue because the bill does not clarify the law. There is a real likelihood that Māori and Pasifika communities will be disproportionately affected by what is a virtually unfettered power to photograph and record for information gathering. The bill's intelligence gathering powers will have a far greater impact on the privacy interests of the community at large than any other kind of information gathering power currently available to law enforcement agencies, and it stands in stark contrast,

in our view, to the careful approach that was taken to the enactment of the Search and Surveillance Act through consultation and routine statutory reviews, which in our view reflects what ought to happen here. Similarly, the approach taken in the bill starkly contrasts with the Criminal Investigations Samples Act, which carefully prescribes the taking and storage of DNA profiles, and that Act's fitness for purpose was recently considered by the Law Commission. It's deeply concerning to us that the bill contains no similar controls for the storage and retention

of personal information. So with those comments in mind, I'll now hand over to Juliet to address you on Part 1. Thank you. Tena koutou katoa. As you've seen, the Law Society has made a range of specific points about Part 1 of the bill, and I'm going to focus on two of those today. The first is to emphasize our view that there are insufficient safeguards in either the bill or the Privacy Act to mitigate privacy risk caused by the expanded intelligence gathering powers that the bill would give to police.

And I suggest this can be seen in two specific mechanisms that are proposed by the bill. The first is proposed Clause 45C, which gives the police very broad powers to collect intelligence on private property without a warrant, and regardless of whether the intelligence being collected has any connection to the reason that the police are on the property in the first place.

No specific safeguards are provided in the bill itself, and it is unclear what the justification for these provisions are, given both the Tamifuna decision and the IPCAOPC report involved images taken in public. The clause, as drafted, amounts to a significant imposition on individual privacy on private property, and the Law Society believes that the development of such a proposal requires clear justification, appropriate safeguards, and specific and adequate public consultation.

The second mechanism that I believe suggests that there are insufficient safeguards is the provisions that enable collection of personal information for intelligence purposes in public. If passed, the bill enables information collection for any lawful purpose connected with a function or activity of the police.

The safeguard and information privacy principle one in the Privacy Act, which says that police may only collect personal information that is necessary for a lawful purpose, will no longer apply. And in addition, as you're aware, the bill provides that police can collect intelligence data not only if it will support a police function at the time of collection, but also if it may support a police function or activity in future.

This is expressed as a limitation on the collection of intelligence, but we suggest that this future-looking, highly permissive limitation will provide only limited protection, in particular, combined with the removal of the necessity requirement. Although the expressed intention is that the Privacy Act will continue to provide protection, as you know, the concept of the purpose of collection of information underpins most of the key information privacy principles in the Privacy Act. Given the permissive nature of both provisions in the bill

that I've referred to, and the fact that these provisions will supersede the information privacy principles, there is a concern that any protection given by the Privacy Act itself would not be adequate. The second point I want to emphasize in the time we have relates to the clarity of the drafting of the provisions. The departmental disclosure statement explains that the bill is intended to address uncertainty created by the Supreme Court in Tamifuna, but the Law Society is concerned that the provisions themselves are drafted in a

way that may create rather than mitigate uncertainty. An example is the provision permitting collection of information for an intelligence purpose connected with a function or activity of the police. As you know, the police's functions are listed in Section 9 of the Policing Act, but neither the word "activity" nor the phrase "intelligence purpose" are defined anywhere. Clarity around the scope of this provision is essential in our view, and that's not only to ensure there's clarity about the impact on individual privacy rights,

but crucially to make sure that frontline police staff understand the limits of their authority to collect information before collection occurs. And we recommend at a minimum that these terms are clarified. Those are the two points I wanted to reiterate in the time available. I will just pass back to Nick to speak to you briefly about part two. Thank you.

Thanks, Juliet. The Law Society's main concern is that whilst part two's framed towards road policing, has the potential application that goes well beyond that to protest, which of course is likely to make it contentious. And one thing I will say is that what we have is essentially a continuation of a power that's been there in some shape or form for nearly five decades.

But now's the time to hit pause and to actually consider the rights implications of this provision, which is another reason why we say that alongside part one, there needs to be some proper consultation with it. And we, of course, in our written submissions, have suggested some preconditions on the use of this new power if it is enacted, which are in paragraph 6.15 and 6.16 of our written submissions. And the reason for that is that this is a power which, of course, is going to be exercised on the ground by what may be junior

police officers. So it would give certainty to the officers involved as well as those whose rights are affected. And the other point, of course, is that what's proposed in terms of use of infringement offenses, we say, would be wrong in principle, given that infringements don't recognize the important rights that are involved and the ability to scrutinize that properly through the court.

Just as a final comment, there has been a recent decision in New South Wales, a case called Jarrett, which was where the changes to the law in relation to the Terrorism Police Powers Act were essentially struck down by the court as being arbitrary and too widely drafted. Whilst a different kind of provision or enactment to what we're dealing with, there were greater controls in place for that legislation. And I'd simply suggest that that does show the risks associated with this type of legislation, which can apply to the freedom of expression and the right to protest.

With that in mind, we're here to answer any questions that you may have for us. We do have a question from Dan Rosewalde. Yeah, thanks, Chair. Just a question for Juliet. In relation to an example that you provided in paragraphs 4.4 of the submission. So a driver is stopped at a routine checkpoint and breath tested.

They pass the test and they haven't committed an offense. So under the bill as drafted, are you saying that police could take a person's photograph and store it indefinitely and for future use in unrelated investigations, even though they've done nothing wrong? Is that what you're saying? That is the Law Society's concern, yes.

That because the taking of the photograph could be interpreted as being a lawful purpose connected with a function or activity of the police, then that would be something that would be information that could be taken using the mechanism under this. The bill is drafted and then stored and used or even disclosed under the Privacy Act, and with very little limitation on that.

Thank you very much, Juliet and Nick, for a very thorough submission today. I'm sorry we've got to move on, but thank you very much. We have that in front of us. Thank you. Next up, we've got the Community Law Centers, Aotearoa in person. Welcome. Come on up. Karen Hodginson. That's me. Huge fan.

Thank you. No Craig today? I'm not sure. He might be joining us, but he was- Okay, so Craig Clark may attend on Zoom May be popping in. He's at a- But welcome. You've got 10 minutes today. Thank you. Excellent. Thank you. Tena tatou. Thank you so much for having me here today. So my name's Karen Hodginson. I'm the law reform coordinator for Community Law Centers, which means that I hear all the feedback from Community Law Centers across the country.

And we do some work in advising people about their engagements with police, and low-level criminal offenses. So our main concern, I'm just going to stick to part one really of the act, I think. Our concerns about part two have really been canvassed by others.

But, oh, kia ora, Craig. Nice to see you. Hi. Sorry I'm a bit late. That's cool. I'm just going to talk to the submissions a little bit, and then Craig can say anything extra or answer any questions as well. So our main concern is that the information collecting powers in this bill are extremely broad. Intelligence purpose is not defined.

There's no proportionality requirement. And it's a circular internal test around what meets the test and what meets intelligence purpose or lawful purpose in Section 45A. There's no requirement to connect it to an investigation. There's no accountability to anybody for why those images and recordings are collected.

No need to get permission from a higher officer or anything like that. The collection point is very broad. Secondly, around retention. And I think a lot of our concerns stem from the drafting of it being such a broad, Section 45 in particular, and those newer sections about collection being so broad, is that then it colors the rest of what happens to this information.

So in terms of retention, because of the very broad purpose in the bill, the retention requirements are really broad as well. So we're talking about this might be useful in the future, so the police could kind of keep this as long as they think necessary, because it might turn up in the future as important or as connecting somebody to something.

And there's no framework. You will have heard from other submitters that once this information is collected by the police, there's nothing else in the bill about how that is used and how it is stored and how it is retained and how it is deleted. And ostensibly, the Privacy Act is supposed to apply here, but again, because of those really broad powers, it will color how the Privacy Act will apply.

We're also concerned about potential impacts on particular groups, Maori and young people in particular, and for the perpetuation of bias. It's really important that there is public confidence in the police and in police powers, and the broad nature of these powers and the lack of framework around them does not give this confidence. I talked about proportionality earlier, but there is a real concern about the broad powers and the broad collection versus the policing value of that information and versus how this is impacting

on privacy rights of people in the public. The Ministry of Justice refers to this, and we understand that there aren't other jurisdictions that we would want to be compared to that actually have these kind of broad powers. I'm not a technical expert on AI, but I think what's really important is that we think about what happens after this is collected. When these sound recordings, these images, are collected and are stored, then what?

We know that there is a greater use of data and of AI across the board, and there's no framework, no discussion in the regulatory impact statement beyond internal police policies to be created that talk about how this data will be mapped with other data, how it will be analyzed, especially taking into account those really broad powers of collection.

We highlight that there are concerns about police IT systems and their ability to sort of manage images and store them and identify them and delete them. I understand from the joint report and the compliance notice, we know that police have done some work to remove the images that were taken and that were identified as needing to be deleted under that joint report.

But there's part of that report where there's parts of the compliance notice that haven't been remedied. And from what I can tell from publicly available information, that there's sort of an inability for police to identify and delete some of these images that were taken unlawfully as a result of this inquiry.

So we're really concerned that that work hasn't been talked about. We don't know if that work's being done, and it's really important that because the rationale for collection is so broad, what are the practical measures in place to ensure the categorization, storage, retention, deletion, and use of this information?

The final point I really want to make is about police integrity and trust and confidence in the police. We make this in our submission. There have been several incidents in recent years and that have damaged, I think, public opinion and public trust and confidence in the police. And this government has a priority about law and order.

But there doesn't seem to be any strong guardrails here for the enforcement of that law and order as it pertains to this particular issue. And we think that law and orderIs about police also knowing what their powers are, having safeguards around those powers to ensure the legitimacy, confidence, and transparency of the enforcement of law and order.

That's really all I have to say. Craig, did you want to emphasize anything or add anything on that? I just made four notes in relation to what's already been said, and I think the vague nature of the drafting has already been well canvassed.

But it is quite concerning that given that currently the majority of the actions of police are subsequently placed under further scrutiny, either through the courts or through the legal community, but the vague nature of this current bill would appear to remove all and any scrutiny because it's not possible to actually define why it is the information's being collected.

And, the fact that the information collection can be justified with reference to a purpose that may come into being at some undefined point in the future, I would suggest is just bad law, and that this bill shouldn't go any further. We're not in a society that countenances the collection of information where there is no clearly defined and lawful purpose.

And I think the vague nature of the drafting would suggest that it's going to be difficult for anyone to define what the purpose of the collection of the information is, let alone frontline police, who are essentially, at the time, adopting a subjective assessment as to some future necessity for the collection of the information in a public place with no detailed parameters prescribing this.

Yeah. That's probably all I have to add, except to say that my background is in residential care and also in the police in the UK, and it was never the case when I was a policeman that decisions could be made that had such potential future implications for an individual without the oversight of someone, inspector or above.

So to suggest that a frontline policeman has sufficient understanding of the implications of the gathering of the information and also the rationale for it, I think is quite naïve. Thank you very much, Craig and Karen.
